componentData.description: Our Senior Leadership team are committed to implementing initiatives, programs and policies which ensure we continually deliver a wide range of effective services to our members.

componentData.title: Join our Membership Community
componentData.description:
Join a global professional community that values people, not just numbers.
At the Institute of Public Accountants (IPA), we’re more than a membership body
— we’re a connected network of professionals who care deeply about helping each
other, our clients, and our communities thrive.

contentColumnItems.title:
As an IPA Member, 
you'll gain:
contentColumnItems.tagline: Our benefits are built to support you

contentColumnItems.description:
• Practical support and expert resources to strengthen your practice and career

  • Professional recognition backed by one of the world’s top accounting bodies

  • Continuing professional development (CPD) opportunities that keep you future-ready

  • Exclusive events and networking with peers who share your passion for public practice

  • Advocacy and representation — we stand up for small business accountants and the profession at large

componentData.description:
Wherever you are in your career — student, emerging practitioner, or experienced professional — there’s a place for you at the IPA.
Join us today and take the next step in your professional journey.

componentData.heading: Subscribe to our newsletter
componentData.description:
Join our 2,000 subscribers to get the latest updates delivered straight to your inbox—no spam, just the good stuff.

  • Stay informed with the latest news, trends, and tips
  • Quick reads designed to fit your busy schedule
  • No spam—ever, just the good stuff

contentColumnItems.title: Professional Practice Program
contentColumnItems.description:
Compulsory for Holders of the Professional Practice Certificate 
The Professional Practice Program is an online self-paced course, consisting of 12 modules including one assessment. Tailored to meet the needs of our trusted advisors – our Members. This program offers a comprehensive guide to the latest professional and ethical standards in the accounting field.

Study Method: 100% Online  Course Duration: 6 Months
Fees: $445 Assessments: 100% Online 
Total Modules: 12 CPD Hours: 42

accordionPanelItems.title: What you'll learn
accordionPanelItems.content:
Designed for accounting practice owners, this program delivers essential knowledge and practical skills to build, manage, and grow a successful firm. You will gain a strong foundation in the accounting profession, ethics, and professional standards, alongside a clear understanding of member responsibilities and regulatory compliance.

The modules guide you through developing a strategic business plan, securing finance, building and managing a team, and implementing effective marketing strategies to attract and retain clients. You will also learn how to protect your practice through strong cyber security practices.

Together, these modules support sustainable growth, operational excellence, and long-term success in today’s evolving accounting landscape

accordionPanelItems.title: Entry Requirements
accordionPanelItems.content: New Professional Practice Certificate (PPC) holders and aspiring PPC holders must attend within six months of receiving their Practicing Certificate. Attending and completing the program is the final step to become a PPC Holder.

accordionPanelItems.title: What You'll Earn
accordionPanelItems.content: Upon completion, you will receive 42 hours of CPD. New Professional Practice Certificate (PPC) holders and aspiring PPC holders are required to attend within six months of receiving their Practising Certificate. Completing this program is the final step to becoming a PPC Holder

accordionPanelItems.title: Program Structure - Course Details
accordionPanelItems.content:
Module:  Topic:
Module 1: Introduction to the accounting profession
Module 2: Ethics and Standards
Module 3 & 4: Members and Members in Practice
Module 5 -7: Statutory Registrations and Regulatory Compliance
Module 8: Strategic Business Plan
Module 9: Financing your practice
Module 10: Staffing your practice
Module 11: Marketing your Practice 
Module 12: Cyber Security

accordionPanelItems.title: Assessments
accordionPanelItems.content: To successfully complete the Professional Practice Program (PPP), attendees must complete all online modules and achieve a passing result in the multiple-choice assessment.

accordionPanelItems.title: Fee Details
accordionPanelItems.content:
Program Fees

Member: $455
Non Member: $495
Students: $445

componentData.title: ABOUT THE PROGRAM
componentData.description: The program is an online self paced course, consisting of 12 modules including one assessment, covering various aspects of professional and ethical standards in accounting. Each module is designed to provide in-depth knowledge and practical tools to identify professional and legal obligations and the key elements of a successful practice.

cardItems.title: Becoming a PPC Holder
cardItems.description: Discover the requirements and steps to becoming a PPC Holder.

componentData.title: Media Releases
componentData.description:
View what we have to say about topics of interest to our members and the accounting profession.
For any queries about the topic of interest, please contact us at media@publicaccountants.org.au.

listingContents.listingTitle: Government Response Falls Short, Small Practitioners Still Carry the Burden
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) has expressed deep concern that the Federal Government’s response to the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Corporations and Financial Services (PJC) report on Ethics and Professional Accountability: Structural Challenges in the Audit, Assurance and Consultancy Industry has failed to adopt key reforms needed to address misconduct in large firms.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA calls for a realistic timeframe for Payday Super reform
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) calls for a pragmatic and phased approach to implementing Payday Super, warning that the current timeline risks overwhelming small employers.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA welcomes changes to Division 296 super tax
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the Government’s announcement of changes to the proposed Division 296 tax and the boost to the Low-Income Superannuation Tax Offset (LISTO). These changes address key concerns raised by the profession.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA: Regulatory burden is a demonstrable drag on the Australian economy
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) warned that Australia's excessive regulatory burden is stifling business dynamism, innovation, and productivity. Following the Productivity Commission’s Five Pillars of Productivity Inquiries’ interim reports, IPA urges the Government to take meaningful steps to reduce red tape.

listingContents.listingTitle: Measuring What Matters: IPA welcomes wellbeing focus but calls for stronger action on small business, education and social inclusion
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomed the broader approach of the Measuring What Matters report to national wellbeing while calling for stronger recognition of small business, sustainability, and the lived experiences of Australians.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA urges the Government to address fundamental flaws in the super tax
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) is urging the Government to reconsider problematic areas with the proposed Division 296 tax on superannuation. While the IPA supports sensible reforms to make the superannuation concessions more equitable, it believes the current proposal will create inequitable outcomes and practical compliance challenges for affected taxpayers.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA warns taxpayers to take urgent steps against identity fraud
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) is urging Australians to take proactive steps to protect themselves from the growing threat of identity fraud, as vulnerabilities in myGov accounts continue to be exploited by cybercriminals.

listingContents.listingTitle: Billions in community grants used to secure election votes, new report reveals
listingContents.listingDescription: A new report has found that billions of dollars in federal community grants have been strategically allocated to secure votes in recent elections, rather than to boost critical community services as intended.

listingContents.listingTitle: 2025-26 Australian Federal Budget: The decline of small business is a silent tax on Australia's future
listingContents.listingDescription: The 2025-26 Federal Budget has failed a critical test: reversing the alarming slide of Australia’s small business sector. Small businesses are confronted with unprecedented challenges. Data reveals a disturbing trend: their contribution to GDP has plummeted from 40 per cent in 2006 to just 33 per cent today, while their share of private sector employment has fallen from 53 per cent to 42 per cent*.

listingContents.listingTitle: 2025-26 Australian Federal Budget: Please don’t ignore the red flags, complacency is our enemy
listingContents.listingDescription: “Australia is sleepwalking into an economic wasteland, and successive governments have been ignoring the red flags,” says Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) CEO Professor Andrew Conway. “Global organisations have been warning Australia about the state of our economy and the lack of attention to productivity boosting reforms and initiatives,” says Professor Conway. Here are some of the disturbing trends we can’t ignore and why we should care.

listingContents.listingTitle: Accounting bodies left with no option but to cease Accounting Professional Year Program for international students
listingContents.listingDescription: CPA Australia, Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand and Institute of Public Accountants have informed the government of their intention to cease the Accounting Professional Year Program (Accounting PY Program).

listingContents.listingTitle: The Institute of Public Accountants' policy priorities to lift Australia’s productivity
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) outlines its policy priorities for the 2025 federal election, heavily focusing on supporting small business productivity and sustainability.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA Calls for a National Small Business Administration Agency to Empower SMEs
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) urges the Australian Government to establish a Small Business Administration (SBA)-style agency to centralise support for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs).

listingContents.listingTitle: Parliamentary Joint Committee on Corporations and Financial Services relating to the inquiry on Ethics and Professional Accountability: Structural Challenges in the Audit, Assurance and Consultancy Industry
listingContents.listingDescription: Overall, the IPA supports the recommendations from the report and believes they will go a long way to restoring trust in the profession, if they are implemented. IPA believes that we must take a holistic and coordinated approach to ensure that we achieve a cohesive and robust outcome in order to restore trust and confidence and to improve accountability and governance.

listingContents.listingTitle: Constitutional changes being proposed at AGM 2024
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) must retain its status as a professional accounting association, in line with government and community expectations. This means the IPA’s complaints and disciplinary function needs to be best practice.

listingContents.listingTitle: Advocacy Update: Amendments to Tax Agent Services (Code of Professional Conduct) Determination 2024 Tabled for Comments
listingContents.listingDescription: There has been significant advocacy work by the profession since the Tax Agent Services (Code of Professional Conduct) Determination 2024 was registered on 2 July 2024, which has resulted in amendments to the determination. Assistant Treasurer Stephen Jones released the amendments to the determination yesterday for public consultation, and submissions are due by 2 October 2024.

listingContents.listingTitle: Institute of Public Accountants welcomes Commonwealth Government climate reporting legislation
listingContents.listingDescription: The Treasury Laws Amendment (Financial Market Infrastructure and Other Measures) Bill 2024 that provides more certainty for climate reporting received Royal Assent on 17 September 2024. The new legislation provides more certainty by establishing when the climate statements should be prepared, how they should be prepared, and which entities should prepare them.

listingContents.listingTitle: Joint Bodies Remain Concerned About New Tas Agent Obligations After Roundtable
listingContents.listingDescription: The professional bodies representing Australia’s tax and BAS agents met with the Assistant Treasurer’s office and Treasury today to discuss our ongoing concerns regarding the Tax Agent Services (Code of Professional Conduct) Determination 2024. The Assistant Treasurer’s office and Treasury acknowledged our concerns with sections 15 and 45 of the Determination and we are pleased they are open to making the necessary changes to address them. However, there is still considerable work to be done and further consultation required.

componentData.title: Advocating for you and the profession
componentData.description: The IPA advocates for its members, the wider profession, and small businesses through lobbying, legislative representation, and thought leadership. It also supports members through special-interest working groups, including sustainability, helping professionals stay informed, collaborate with peers, and contribute to shaping the future of the profession.

contentColumnItems.title: Important Notification
contentColumnItems.description: The new anti-money laundering / counter-terrorism funding (AML/CTF) tranche 2 reforms will come into effect for professional accountants from 1 July 2026. Discover our assets and guides to assist accountants through these changes>

componentData.title: How does our Advocacy team act as your voice?
Submissions
componentData.description: The Institute of Public Accountants develops policy submissions to government and regulators to advocate for its members, influence legislation, and ensure the interests of accountants, small businesses, and the broader profession are represented in decision-making.

listingContents.listingTitle: AASB ED 338 Application of AASB 18 and AASB 107 by Superannuation and Not-for-Profit Entities and Operating Cash Flow Reconciliation
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure Draft (ED). Overall, IPA supports the proposals contained in ED 338.

listingContents.listingTitle: Joint Association Submission: Enhancing oversight and governance of managed investment schemes
listingContents.listingDescription: Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand (CA ANZ), CPA Australia, the Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) and the SMSF Association (the Joint Associations) welcome the opportunity to provide this submission to the Treasury consultation on Enhancing oversight and governance of managed investment schemes.

listingContents.listingTitle: Joint Association Submission: Enhancing the effectiveness of financial services professional indemnity insurance
listingContents.listingDescription: Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand (CA ANZ), CPA Australia, the Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) and the SMSF Association (the Joint Associations) welcome the opportunity to provide this submission to the Treasury Consultation on Enhancing the effectiveness of financial service professional indemnity insurance.

listingContents.listingTitle: ASIC CS 42 Proposed update to ASIC’s guidance on Regulatory Guidance (RG) 43 Financial reports and audit relief
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on CS 42. IPA commends ASIC in its ongoing commitment to simplify financial, markets and corporate regulations and guidance documents. We have observed the improvements in other Regulatory Guidance, such as RG 280 Sustainability Reporting, are well structured and have easy to understand content.

listingContents.listingTitle: Statutory review of the electric car discount
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the statutory review of the electric car discount.

listingContents.listingTitle: ATO Draft Rental Property Guidance
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the ATO Draft Property Rental Guidance. We welcome the ATO’s efforts to modernise the approach taken in IT 2167.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA Pre-Budget Submission 2026-27
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) Group welcomes the opportunity to present our pre-Budget submission for the 2026-27 financial year. We look forward to working with the Government on its economic agenda set against the challenges of an uncertain domestic and international environment.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA Tax Reform Policy Paper 2026
listingContents.listingDescription: Tax reform has stalled in Australia, in part because most tax discussions have been the subject of political trench warfare. Partisan arguments over reforms will usually result in no change, unless a government has the necessary numbers in both houses of the Federal Parliament to successfully shepherd through reform without the need for unnecessary compromise. Tax reform has been a key part of successive governments’ policy agenda to create a tax system that supports higher economic growth and living standards, improves international competitiveness and adjusts to a changing economy, yet there has been little progress to achieve these stated aims.

listingContents.listingTitle: Treasury Laws Amendment (Better Targeted Superannuation Concessions) Bill 2025
listingContents.listingDescription: Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand, CPA Australia and the Institute of Public Accountants (“the joint bodies”) welcome the opportunity to comment on the consultation for the exposure draft legislation Treasury Laws Amendment (Better Targeted Superannuation Concessions) Bill 2025 (the “BTSC Bill”) and the Superannuation (Better Targeted Superannuation Concessions) Imposition Bill 2025 (the “Imposition Bill”) (together, the “draft Bills”), related explanatory materials and additional guidance (“the material”).

listingContents.listingTitle: AUASB Exposure Draft: Proposed Australian Standard on Sustainability Assurance ASSA 2025-11 – Amendments to ASSA 5000 General Requirements for Sustainability Assurance Engagements
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure draft (ED 03/25).

listingContents.listingTitle: Treasury Exposure Draft (ED): Treasury Laws Amendment Bill 2025: Financial Reporting System Reform
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above ED.

listingContents.listingTitle: Draft Practical Compliance Guideline PCG 2025/D5 Payday Super
listingContents.listingDescription: The Joint Bodies appreciate the opportunity to comment on PCG 2025/D5 and support the ATO’s risk-based compliance approach for the first year of Payday Super. Given the scale of change and reliance on third-party systems, we strongly recommend extending the transitional compliance period and providing clearer guidance on key concepts such as ‘reasonably practicable’ and Voluntary Disclosure Statements (VDSs). We also propose the introduction of ATO-led nudge messaging to assist employers in understanding SG payment timing and system performance during the transition year.

listingContents.listingTitle: ASIC Report 813 Regulatory simplification (REP 813)
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on REP 813.

listingContents.listingTitle: Tax Ombudsman review: ATO’s management of remission of the general interest charge
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the review: ATO’s management of remission of the general interest charge.

listingContents.listingTitle: Guidance on best practice principles for superannuation retirement income solutions (“Consultation Paper”)
listingContents.listingDescription: Chartered Accountants Australia & New Zealand (CA ANZ), CPA Australia, the Institute of Public Accountants, and the SMSF Association welcome the opportunity to make a submission to the Consultation Paper. Shifting the focus of Australia’s retirement system from accumulating superannuation to that of drawing income in retirement is a large cultural shift. We acknowledge the government’s attempts to encourage this.

listingContents.listingTitle: Treasury Consultation Paper: Climate-related Transition Planning Guide
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Consultation Paper. IPA commends Treasury in developing a transition planning guidance. The success of correctly complying with the mandatory climate disclosures depends on the reporting entities’ ability to understand and apply the requirements accurately and consistently.

listingContents.listingTitle: Invitation to Comment (ITC) 55 Post-Implementation Review of AASB 16 Leases
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above ITC. Overall, IPA is of the view that the costs and benefits of the leases Standard are largely as the IASB expected. That is, the Standard has met its objective and its core principles are clear and improve the quality and comparability of financial information about leases, while the initial implementation cost was higher compared to the on-going costs of applying the requirements.

listingContents.listingTitle: Compensation Scheme of Last Resort (“CSLR”): Exceeding sub-sector levy cap
listingContents.listingDescription: Chartered Accountants Australia & New Zealand (CAANZ), CPA Australia and the Institute of Public Accountants welcome the opportunity to make a submission to the consultation paper CSLR: exceeding sub-sector levy cap in the context of the post-implementation review of the CSLR announced by the Government on 31 January 2025.

listingContents.listingTitle: Economic Reform Roundtable
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission to the Government’s upcoming Economic Reform Roundtable.

listingContents.listingTitle: Draft ATO Vulnerability Framework
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the ATO’s draft Vulnerability Framework (the Framework).

listingContents.listingTitle: AUASB Consultation Paper: IAASB’s Proposed Narrow-Scope Amendments to IAASB Standards Arising from the IESBA’s Using the Work of an External Expert Project
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Consultation Paper (CP). Overall, IPA supports the proposed narrow-scope amendments in the CP for multiple reasons.

listingContents.listingTitle: AFCA Rules – Proposed amendments
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'AFCA Rules – Proposed amendments'.

Overview of the Submission:

Chartered Accountants ANZ, CPA Australia, the Financial Advice Association Australia (FAAA), the Institute of Public Accountants and the SMSF Association welcome the opportunity to provide feedback to AFCA in response to the Consultation paper on the proposed amendments to the AFCA Rules. We appreciate that some of these changes are being made to reflect changes to the law or to address the historical nature of the Legacy complaints provisions. We also recognise that some of these changes are sensibly being done to provide AFCA with additional powers to deal with AFCA members and third parties who are not complying with the expectations of AFCA. These are relevant matters that we believe should be addressed.

listingContents.listingTitle: Five Productivity Inquiries
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Five Productivity Inquiries'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the Productivity Commission’s five productivity inquiries. We welcome the Government’s commitment to lifting Australia’s productivity and the commissioning of these inquiries. Fundamental reforms aimed at boosting the rate of productivity growth are overdue. We urge the Government to take a longer-term view based on a clear, determined and well communicated path for the Australian economy and Australian society.

listingContents.listingTitle: Australian National Audit Office (ANAO) Performance Audit – Australian Securities and Investments Commission’s (ASIC) regulation of registered company auditors
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Australian National Audit Office (ANAO) Performance Audit – Australian Securities and Investments Commission’s (ASIC) regulation of registered company auditors'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) is one of the three professional accounting bodies in Australia, having been established in 1923, and represents over 50,000 accountants, business advisers, academics, and students throughout Australia and internationally. Three quarters of the IPA’s members work in or are advisers to small business and Small to Medium Enterprises (SMEs). IPA welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the ANAO’s Performance Audit on the effectiveness of ASIC’s regulation of registered company auditors (RCA).

listingContents.listingTitle: APESB ED 01/25 Proposed Australian Ethics Standards for Sustainability Assurance and Other Revisions to the Code
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'APESB ED 01/25 Proposed Australian Ethics Standards for Sustainability Assurance (including Independence Standards) and Other Revisions to the Code Relating to Sustainability Assurance and Reporting'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure Draft (ED). Overall, IPA supports the proposals in ED 01/25 to amend the APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (including Independence Standards) (the Code) to incorporate new ethical standards for Australian sustainability assurance practitioners and professional accountants relating to sustainability reporting and assurance.

listingContents.listingTitle: APESB ED 02/25 Proposed Revisions to APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (including Independence Standards) Addressing Using the Work of an External Expert
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'APESB ED 02/25 Proposed Revisions to APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (including Independence Standards) Addressing Using the Work of an External Expert'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure Draft (ED).
Overall, IPA supports the proposals in ED 02/25 to amend the existing APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants (including Independence Standards) (the Code) to guide professional accountants in both business and public practice when using the work of an External Expert.

listingContents.listingTitle: IGTO 2026 Draft Workplan for Systemic Reviews
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'IGTO 2026 Draft Workplan for Systemic Reviews'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the IGTO’s 2026 draft workplan for systemic reviews. The IPA is one of the three professional accounting bodies in Australia, representing over 50,000 members and students in Australia and in over 100 countries. Approximately three-quarters of the IPA’s members work in or are advisers to small business and small to medium enterprises.

listingContents.listingTitle: Payday super – exposure draft legislation
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Payday super – exposure draft legislation'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Australian Bookkeepers Association, Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand, CPA Australia, the Financial Advice Association of Australia, the Institute of Certified Bookkeepers, the Institute of Public Accountants, the SMSF Association and The Tax Institute (together, the Joint Bodies) write to you as the peak professional accounting and tax practitioner bodies in Australia representing the tax profession, the superannuation sector and financial advisers. We welcome the opportunity to make a submission to the Treasury regarding its consultation on the package of exposure draft legislation on payday super (PDS).

listingContents.listingTitle: Proposed Financial Institutions Supervisory Levies for 2025-26
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Proposed Financial Institutions Supervisory Levies for 2025-26'.

Overview of the Submission:

CPA Australia, Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand (CA ANZ) and the Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) (“the Joint Accounting Bodies”) welcome the opportunity to provide a submission to Treasury on the Proposed Financial Institutions Supervisory Levies for 2025-26 Discussion Paper (“the Discussion Paper”), currently available for consultation. Together, the Joint Accounting Bodies represent over 350,000 professional accountants in Australia, New Zealand and around the world. Our members work in diverse roles across public practice, commerce, industry, government, and academia. We make this submission on behalf of our members and in the broader public interest.

listingContents.listingTitle: Australia’s circular economy: Unlocking the opportunities
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Australia’s circular economy: Unlocking the opportunities'.

Overview of the Submission:

The IPA supports the proposals outlined in the interim report and commends the effort to advance the circular economy agenda in Australia. We firmly believe that increased investment and comprehensive research in this field are beneficial and essential. Australia has lagged behind international counterparts in adopting and implementing robust circular economy initiatives and regulations, and decisive action is now critical to rectify this. The transition towards a circular economy presents significant opportunities for innovation, economic growth, and environmental sustainability, and we urge a proactive and ambitious approach. However, there is further research and guidance required.

listingContents.listingTitle: Review of ATO letters and written communications
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Review of ATO letters and written communications'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the IGTO’s Review of ATO letters and written communications.

From the feedback we have received, we note the following key issues:
• There have been instances where taxpayers have received written communications as well as their tax agent.
• The ATO needs to improve their review procedures before communications are issued to ensure that taxpayers and their tax agents do not receive correspondence in error.
• Clearer wording as to the purpose of a communication may be necessary.
• Lack of a contact person noted in the correspondence means that practitioners find it difficult to discuss the issue with an ATO officer who has expert knowledge about the issue.

listingContents.listingTitle: Systemic review of the effectiveness of the ATO’s registered agent phone line
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Systemic review of the effectiveness of the ATO’s registered agent phone line'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the IGTO’s Systemic review of the effectiveness of the ATO’s registered agent phone line. The IPA is one of the three professional accounting bodies in Australia, representing over 50,000 members and students in Australia and in over 100 countries. Approximately three-quarters of the IPA’s members work in or are advisers to small business and small to medium enterprises.

listingContents.listingTitle: TR 2013/5 and Compliance Concerns
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'TR 2013/5 and Compliance Concerns'.

Overview of the Submission:

Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand, CPA Australia, Institute of Financial Professionals Australia, Institute of Public Accountants, and the SMSF Association (together the (‘Joint Bodies’) appreciate the ATO’s engagement and thank you for your response dated 24 January 2025 regarding the industry’s concerns with the updates to Taxation Ruling TR 2013/5(the Ruling).

Whilst we acknowledge that the Ruling can only deal with matters relating to tax law, it is critical that it is acknowledged that there are broader consequences for all superannuation fund trustees, members and the sector as a whole.

Outlined below are some key issues. We seek the opportunity to engage in further dialogue to ensure practical, equitable outcomes for all those affected.

listingContents.listingTitle: Brief: Treasury Laws Amendment (Tax Incentives and Integrity) Bill 2024
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Brief: Treasury Laws Amendment (Tax Incentives and Integrity) Bill 2024'.

Overview of the Submission:

This brief provides a short overview of concerns held by industry in relation to Treasury Laws Amendment (Tax Incentives and Integrity) Bill 2024 (the Bill). The brief is authored by Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand (CA ANZ), CPA Australia (CPA), Institute of Public Accountants (IPA), National Tax and Accountants Association (NTAA) and Institute of Certified Bookkeepers (ICB).

listingContents.listingTitle: AASB ED 334 Limiting the Ability of Not-for-Profit Entities to Prepare Special Purpose Financial Statements
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'AASB ED 334 Limiting the Ability of Not-for-Profit Entities to Prepare Special Purpose Financial Statements'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure Draft (ED).

GENERAL COMMENT
Overall, IPA supports the proposals in ED 334:
• that essentially amends the Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting (Conceptual Framework) to extend the application to not-for-profit (NFP) private and public sector entities that meet the criteria of preparing financial statements that comply with Australian Accounting Standards or accounting standards, or if the entity elects to prepare general purpose financial statements and
• that the above amendment will result in the Framework for the Preparation and Presentation of Financial Statements and SAC 1 Definition of the Reporting Entity being superseded for an entity when the Conceptual Framework is applicable.

listingContents.listingTitle: AASB ED 335 General Purpose Financial Statements – Not-for-Profit Private Sector Tier 3 Entities
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'AASB ED 335 General Purpose Financial Statements – Not-for-Profit Private Sector Tier 3 Entities'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to provide comments on the above Exposure Draft (ED).

GENERAL COMMENT
IPA commends the AASB undertaking the project to simplify GPSF for smaller Tier 3 entities in a single stand-alone accounting standard. IPA therefore supports the overall objectives and principles of the draft standard. In particular, we support the proposals to reduce the accounting choices in areas such as:
• Financial instrument simplification (Q16)
• Practical expedient for identifying the highest and best use of non-financial assets (Q19)
• Specific identification of inventory cost of FIFO or weighted average and prohibiting LIFO (Q20(b))
• Expensing borrowing costs (Q31) and
• Modified retrospective approach and when it is impracticable to determine the cumulative effect of applying a new accounting policy (Q15).

listingContents.listingTitle: Review of Tax Regulator Secrecy Exceptions
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Review of Tax Regulator Secrecy Exceptions'.

Overview of the Submission:

CPA Australia, Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand and the Institute of Public Accountants (we/our/joint bodies) together with our respective affiliate bodies represent over 350,000 professional accountants in Australia, New Zealand and around the world. We submit our comments on the Review of Tax Regulator Secrecy Exceptions (Review) consultation paper (consultation paper).

listingContents.listingTitle: Consultation Paper: Positioning Australia’s financial reporting system for the future
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'Consultation Paper: Positioning Australia’s financial reporting system for the future'.

Overview of the Submission:

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) provided a submission to the Treasury regarding the consultation paper on positioning Australia's financial reporting system for the future. Overall, the IPA supported the proposed structure and principles but expressed concerns regarding the timing, level of detail, competencies, and independence of the new body. The IPA emphasised the importance of transparency, accountability, and functional separation between the governing board and technical standard-setting committees. The IPA suggested more public transparency in deliberations and expressed concern regarding the role of external experts, potential conflicts of interest, and Board member termination guidelines. The IPA also commented on the eligibility requirements for board and committee appointments and suggested strengthening institutional governance.

listingContents.listingTitle: IPA Federal Election Policy Priorities 2025
listingContents.listingDescription:
Please download to view the Submission 'IPA Federal Election Policy Priorities 2025'.

Overview of the Submission:

Essential election policies to lift Australia’s productivity.

Robust visionary policy reform and long-term support for small business underpinned by economic and environmental sustainability are crucial to revitalise productivity and grow the Australian economy.

All sides of politics refer to small businesses as the backbone of the Australian economy, yet they face excessive regulatory burdens, fragmented government support, and difficulties accessing capital.

The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) calls on the next Parliament to focus on four key policy areas that will significantly improve small business productivity and sustainability and lift the Australian economy.

listingContents.listingDescription: Please download to view the Submission 'Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Rules 2024 (Exposure Draft): First round'.

listingContents.listingTitle: Mandating cash acceptance — consultation paper
listingContents.listingDescription: The Institute of Public Accountants (IPA) welcomes the opportunity to make a submission in relation to the Consultation Paper titled Mandating cash acceptance (the Paper). The IPA is one of the three professional accounting bodies in Australia, representing over 50,000 members and students in Australia and in over 100 countries. Approximately three-quarters of the IPA’s members work in or are advisers to small business and small to medium enterprises.

componentData.title: Your Migration Assessment
componentData.description:
All the information you need to submit your application for your Migration Qualification & Skill Assessments with the IPA
Jump to

^^The Review fee will be refundable if the assessment outcome is overturned. You must lodge an application for review within 60 days after the assessment date.

contentColumnItems.title: Payment Methods
contentColumnItems.description:
• Credit Cards - All major credit card providers

  • Overseas Bank Draft - The draft should be made payable to the Institute of Public Accountants and nominate a corresponding Australian bank through which payment can be processed

  • Australian Bank Cheque - made payable to the Institute of Public Accountants

  • Australia Post Money Order - made payable to the Institute of Public Accountants.

contentColumnItems.title: Processing Times
contentColumnItems.description:
Priority Applications
Our Priority service guarantees a response within 1 - 2 business days of receiving your application. You can apply for Priority service if you need the outcome for Qualification Assessment / Skilled Employment Assessment urgently.
Standard Applications
We provide responses for standard Qualification Assessment applications / Skilled Employment Assessment applications within 7 business days of receiving your application. The processing time is indicative only and can change due to the volume of applications received.

contentColumnItems.title: Required Documentation
contentColumnItems.description:
Supporting documents must be clear, high-quality colour copies.

Certification is not required, as the Department of Home Affairs (DHA) will conduct further checks later in the migration process. If false or fraudulent information is identified, IPA will issue an unsuitable outcome letter, report the matter to DHA, and may impose a temporary ban on future skills assessment applications.

Required documents:

  • Passport ID page, clearly showing your face and all other details on the page
  • University Degree Certificates (or an official completion letter if you have only recently graduated with an Australian degree)
  • Official academic transcripts or mark sheets, showing your individual grades. More information>
  • Official syllabus details / subject outlines of your university course studied outside Australia; syllabus details for Australian qualification are only required upon request.
  • Valid English language test results
  • Secondary / High School certificates and mark sheets (only required if your degree was completed from a Subcontinental country)
  • If you have changed your name since studying, please provide a Marriage certificate or other formal evidence to support your claim

Please note that it is your responsibility to provide formal translated copies of all documents to us, if the original document is not in English. If you require translating service, please contact NAATI.

Additional required documents for Skilled Employment Assessments:

  • References on company or government department providing the employment reference. See reference requirements and alternate options below.
  • Contracts of Employment
  • Tax Returns
  • Payslips

accordionPanelItems.title: What are the requirements for employment references?
accordionPanelItems.content:
Please attach high-quality colour scans of work references on the letterhead of the company or government department providing the employment reference.

The letterhead should:

  1. Show clearly the full address of the company, telephone and fax numbers, email and website address.
  2. Include the name and position of the referee signing the employment reference, typed or stamped below that referee's signature. The signatory must be your line manager, CEO or HR Manager. A reference where the details of the signatory are illegible will not be accepted.
  3. Include the direct contact details of the referee.

The reference must include:

  1. the length of time you were employed;
  2. whether the role was permanent, temporary, full or part-time;
  3. for part-time employees, the number of hours worked per week;
  4. your hourly rate;
  5. your main duties and responsibilities; and
  6. your job title/position.

Other documents to support your claim may include:

  1. Contracts of employment.
  2. Tax returns
  3. Payslips

References for Australian and overseas work experience must relate to employment that is no more than ten years old at the time of lodging your visa application.

For further information on how points are awarded for both overseas and Australian skilled employment, please refer to SkillSelect.

accordionPanelItems.title: If I'm not able to provide employer work references what other evidence is acceptable?
accordionPanelItems.content:
You may provide a work reference from a senior colleague in the same organisation with expertise closely related to your nominated occupation. This reference must be on or submitted with a Statutory Declaration. A Statutory Declaration is a legal document to be signed and witnessed by an authorised person.

Please include on the Statutory Declaration the following words:
I understand that a person who intentionally makes a false statement in a statutory declaration is guilty of an offence, and I believe that the statements in this declaration are true in every particular.
Requirements for this evidence type:
  1. The referee must verify their employment and position. This may be by way of their business card, payslips and other relevant evidence.
  2. The work reference / Statutory Declaration must include full employer details: i.e. name, address, telephone and fax numbers, email and website addresses.
  3. Information to be provided is per reference guidelines.
  4. Please note the Institute of Public Accountants is under no obligation to accept references provided by colleagues.

Providing fraudulent documents or declarations carries serious consequences and will be reported to the Department of Home Affairs (DHA).

accordionPanelItems.title: I am/was self-employed. What evidence do I provide?
accordionPanelItems.content:
You may provide a Statutory Declaration with a detailed explanation of the nature of your business.

Please include on the Statutory Declaration the following words: 
I understand that a person who intentionally makes a false statement in a statutory declaration is guilty of an offence, and I believe that the statements in this declaration are true in every particular.

The following documents must also be attached:

  • Certificate of Business Registration
  • Tax Returns for your business
  • References/recommendations from business associates (with business cards attached)
  • Contracts for work undertaken
  • Any other information that demonstrates relevant skilled employment in the course of self-employment.

If you worked as a contractor through an agency, a reference from the agency is acceptable. This reference must detail the nature of contracts, the periods of your contracts, and your key duties and responsibilities.

accordionPanelItems.title: My employer pays me cash and I don’t get payslips. How can I prove my employment?
accordionPanelItems.content:
Cash-in-hand” or “off-the-books” are phrases used to describe a job where your employer pays you in cash to avoid paying you any of your entitlements, like superannuation or public holiday rates. It’s also frequently used by employers to try and stay off the radar of authorities like Fair Work Australia and the Australian Tax Office. By paying cash in hand, employers deny their workers their public holiday and weekend rates (also known as penalty rates), superannuation, and general workplace rights.

Your employer must provide you with a payslip every pay period, as well as a payment summary at the end of every financial year. No matter how you’re paid – cash or bank transfer – your employer must provide you with a detailed payslip within one day of the end of every pay period. A pay slip must include (amongst other things) your total pay before and after tax, your hourly rate and any penalty rates that apply (and the number of hours worked at those rates) and any deductions (such as tax or superannuation) that came out of your pay. Your employer must also provide you with a group certificate or payment summary at the end of every financial year, detailing your total income from that employer and the total amount of tax your employer withheld from you.

We will not accept or assess Skilled Employment whereby cash is paid without valid regular payslips and payment summaries provided to support your application.

contentColumnItems.title: English Requirements
contentColumnItems.description:
For a General Skilled Migration qualifications assessment, it is an Australian Government requirement and guarantees you points when applying to Department of Home Affairs. This includes English speaking countries such as the USA or the UK.
You must provide evidence of a completed English test, with the following minimum band scores for tests taken from 7 August 2025 onwards.
Test Component Pearson (PTE) Academic IELTS Academic* TOEFL iBT^ Cambridge (CAE)
Listening 58 7.0 22 175
Reading 59 7.0 22 179
Writing 69 7.0 26 193
Speaking 76 7.0 24 194

*We are accepting IELTS Academic One Skill Retake (OSR) result from May 2023. Please provide both your standard IELTS Academic test report and OSR result for assessment. OSR result is not accepted for some visa subclasses. Please check the English language visa requirements directly with Department of Home Affairs.

^ TOEFL iBT scores from tests taken between 26 July 2023 and 4 May 2024 are not acceptable for qualification assessment. During this period, TOEFL iBT was not a recognised test approved by the Department of Home Affairs. 

Please click here for scores requirement if your test was undertaken before 7 August 2025.

When applying, you must submit a high-quality colour scan of your test results. We'll complete the necessary security checks to validate and confirm your results.

We accept:

  • Pearson PTE / IELTS tests undertaken within the past three years if the score reports can be verified with the providers.
  • TOEFL / Cambridge CAE tests undertaken within the past three years.
  • Completion of Accounting Professional Year Program (Accounting PYP) as an alternative to formal English testing.

Please contact us if your English test result expires in less than 30 days.

contentColumnItems.title: Refunds
contentColumnItems.description:
Refunds are available only if we have not commenced work on your application, including the review of supporting documents. In this case, a refund of the total fee minus the administration fee will apply.

It is your responsibility to submit all required supporting documents at the time of application. Refunds will not be issued if required documents or information are missing or cannot be provided. Please ensure all documents listed in the documentation checklist are gathered before submitting your assessment.

No refunds are offered for applications that have been assessed and returned with a negative outcome. Assessment fees do not guarantee a successful outcome.

Disclaimer*

Paying the assessment fee does not guarantee that your assessment will be successful. The fee covers the time and resources required to assess your application.
In cases where the IPA assesses your qualifications and/or skilled employment as suitable for the nominated occupation, the final decision regarding your visa application, including the awarding of points, is made by the Department of Home Affairs (DHA). The IPA has no part in DHA’s decision-making process.
The IPA does not offer advice on visa or migration requirements. You may contact a registered migration agent if you need assistance in dealing with migration related matters.

accordionPanelItems.title: I lost my paper-based outcome letter, and require a letter for migration purposes, what should I do?
accordionPanelItems.content: Click here to request a replacement letter. You will have 90 days to access and download the letter, after which time your authorised access will expire. Please note that an administration fee of $90 will apply.

accordionPanelItems.title: Will I receive a successful assessment outcome if I have met IPA or IFA’s membership requirements to become a member?
accordionPanelItems.content: The assessment criteria for migration purpose are different to the professional bodies’ membership requirements. Being a member of the IPA or IFA does not guarantee a suitable outcome for migration assessment. You are assessed based on the educational standards outlined in Qualification Assessments.

accordionPanelItems.title: Can I claim points for both overseas and Australian skilled employment?
accordionPanelItems.content: Yes, we will assess and provide advice on your skilled employment both in Australia and overseas. Department of Home Affairs (DHA) will take this into consideration when making a decision on awarding the points. IPA has no part in DHA's decision making on this matter.

accordionPanelItems.title: Can I submit additional information for an appeal or reassessment?
accordionPanelItems.content: No. The assessment is based on information, references and other supporting evidence provided with your initial application. To prevent against fraud and document manipulation, we will not accept altered references at any time after you have lodged the initial application. We do not reassess skilled employment outcome after the decision has been made. This means all the required supporting documentation or information must be submitted before the decision is made for your skilled employment assessment.

accordionPanelItems.title: Can I submit my application if I am still studying my course at Australian university?
accordionPanelItems.content: No. You must wait until you have finished your studies. You will need to provide the letter of completion and full official academic transcript with your application as evidence of completion.

accordionPanelItems.title: As an International Student how can I be sure that the DHA will accept my PR visa application?
accordionPanelItems.content: The IPA has been advised by DHA that graduates will be able to use this updated assessment to apply for general skilled migration. Please check with DHA or a migration agent for more information, or if you have queries regarding your personal situation.

accordionPanelItems.title: Where should I have my qualifications verified if my bachelor degree is from China?
accordionPanelItems.content: All Chinese qualifications can be verified by Center for Student Services and Development, Ministry of Education, P.R. China (CSSD) in China or VETASSESS in Australia. Your Bachelor Degree Certificate and Academic Transcript in both Chinese and English translation must be submitted for verification. When submitting your qualifications to us, please include a copy of the original report attached with the certificate and transcript in both Chinese and English translation.
